title: "Crimson Orb Puzzle v10913"

description: |
  At 22:15 the Crimson Orb vanished from a locked pedestal.
  Four badge-holders (1 2 3 4) were nearby.
  Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements; the other three speak only truths.
  Exactly one suspect is guilty.

  Suspect 1:
    (1) "If $L4$ then $G4$." $L4 \to G4$
    (2) "If $T2$ then $P4$." $T2 \to P4$
    (3) "$\lnot L3$."
    (4) "If $G4$ then $L2$." $G4 \to L2$

  Suspect 2:
    (1) "$P4$."
    (2) "If $L3$ then $L1$." $L3 \to L1$
    (3) "$G4 \oplus L1$."
    (4) "If $T1$ then $\lnot P3$." $T1 \to \lnot P3$

  Suspect 3:
    (1) "$\lnot P3$."
    (2) "If $T4$ then $P2$." $T4 \to P2$
    (3) "$L2 \lor G4$."

  Suspect 4:
    (1) "$\lnot G4$."
    (2) "$P3$."
    (3) "$L1 \land P1$."
    (4) "$P2 \oplus L2$."

  Notation:
    $Lx$ = “x lies in every statement”. $Tx$ = “x is entirely truthful”.
    $Gx$ = “x is guilty”. $Px$ = “x was present at 22:15”.
    $\lnot$ = negation, $\land$ = and, $\lor$ = or, $\oplus$ = exclusive or, $\to$ = implication.

question: "Who stole the Crimson Orb? Return a single digit (1–4)."

answer: >
  explanation: |
    Assume each suspect is the sole liar (exactly one $Lx$ true).

    Case $L1$  
      1(1) requires $L4 \to G4$ to be false, so $L4$ true and $G4$ false.  
      That makes $L4$ a second liar. Two liars contradict the rules. Reject.

    Case $L2$  
      2(2) is false, so $L3$ true and $L1$ false.  Now both $L2$ and $L3$ are liars. Reject.

    Case $L3$  
      3(1) false → $P3$ true.  
      3(2) false → $T4$ true and $P2$ false.  
      With $T4$ true, 4(2) (“$P3$”) must be true, but $P3$ is true only if 4 tells the truth, contradicting $L3$ as sole liar. Reject.

    Case $L4$  
      All four statements of suspect 4 are false, giving  
        $\lnot G4$ false → $G4$ true;  
        $P3$ false -> $\lnot P3$ true;  
        $L1 \land P1$ false → $L1$ false;  
        $P2 \oplus L2$ false → $P2$ and $L2$ have equal truth value (both false here).  
      Now $T1$,$T2$,$T3$ are truthful.  
        - 1(1): $L4$ true and $G4$ true → statement true.  
        - 1(2): $T2$ true implies $P4$; 2(1) gives $P4$ true, so statement true.  
        - 1(3): $\lnot L3$ true.  
        - 1(4): $G4$ true implies $L2$; 2 is truthful, so $L2$ false and the conditional holds.  
        - 2(1): $P4$ true.  
        - 2(2): $L3$ false makes implication true.  
        - 2(3): $G4$ true, $L1$ false → xor true.  
        - 2(4): $T1$ true and $\lnot P3$ true → implication true.  
        - 3(1): $\lnot P3$ true.  
        - 3(2): $T4$ false, so implication vacuously true.  
        - 3(3): $L2$ false, $G4$ true → disjunction true.  

      Consistent with exactly one liar ($L4$) and exactly one guilty ($G4$).

    Only $L4$ remains possible, forcing $G4$.

  final: '4'
